claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and in approved education
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child works 24+ hours/week but is in approved education
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 17 and in approved education; Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child works 24+ hours/week but is in approved education; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 17 and in approved education
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child works 24+ hours/week but is in approved education
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin. To determine your eligibility for Child Benefit, I need to ask you some questions about your child.

**First question: How old is your child?**

The eligibility criteria depend on the child's age:
- **Under 16**: Generally automatically qualifies
- **16 to 19**: May qualify if in approved education or training
- **20 or over**: Generally not eligible

Please tell me your child's age, and if you have multiple children you'd like to check eligibility for, let me know about each of them.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Thanks for helping me with this. I have one child - my son Alex. He's 17 years old and lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! So you have one child, Alex, who is 17 years old and lives at home with you.

Since Alex is 17 (which falls in the 16-19 age range), his eligibility will depend on whether he's in approved education or training. Let me navigate to the next step.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect. Now I need to know about Alex's education or training status.

**Is Alex currently:**
1. **In approved education or training**, or
2. **Has he recently left education or training** (within the last 20 weeks), or
3. **Neither** - not in education/training and didn't recently leave

Could you tell me which situation applies to Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex is currently in education. He's studying for his A-levels at sixth form college.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Alex is studying for his A-levels at sixth form college, which is approved education. Let me move to the next step to verify the education level requirements.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great news! A-levels at sixth form college are considered **approved (non-advanced) education**, which meets the requirements for Child Benefit eligibility for 16-19 year olds.

Now I need to check a few more details about Alex's education:

1. **Is his course full-time?** (This means at least 12 hours per week of supervised study)
2. **Is this education funded or arranged by his employer**, or does he have an agreement with an employer for a future job as part of his course?

For A-levels at sixth form, these are typically full-time and not employer-funded, but I need to confirm with you.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, it's full-time - he's there during the day like a regular school schedule. And no, it's not funded by any employer or anything like that. He's just doing his A-levels at the local sixth form college.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! That's exactly what I needed to know. A-levels at sixth form college on a full-time basis (not employer-funded) are approved education for Child Benefit purposes. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now I need to check Alex's work status.

**Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work at all)?**

For children aged 16-19 in approved education, they can still qualify for Child Benefit as long as they're not working 24 hours or more per week.

Does Alex work fewer than 24 hours per week, or is he not working at all?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He does work, actually. He has a part-time job at the local supermarket - it's pretty much full-time hours alongside his studies. He works quite a lot to save up money.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I need to clarify this important detail because it affects Alex's eligibility.

**How many hours per week does Alex work at the supermarket?**

Specifically, is it:
- **Less than 24 hours per week**, or
- **24 hours or more per week**?

You mentioned it's "pretty much full-time hours" - if he's working 24 hours or more per week, this would affect his eligibility for Child Benefit, even though he's in approved education.

Could you tell me approximately how many hours per week he works?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Oh, I see what you're asking. Yes, it's 24 hours or more - he works quite a bit, basically full-time hours at the supermarket while doing his A-levels. He's been managing both.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for clarifying that. Let me navigate to the next step based on this information.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I understand the situation now. Unfortunately, I need to inform you of the outcome based on the Child Benefit rules.

**You are NOT eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

**The reason:** Although Alex is in approved education (A-levels), children aged 16 or over who work 24 or more hours per week do not qualify for Child Benefit, even if they're still studying.

**Important guidance:** 
- Child Benefit stops when a child aged 16 or over starts paid work for 24+ hours per week
- If Alex reduces his working hours to less than 24 hours per week while continuing his A-level studies, you may be able to claim Child Benefit again
- Once he completes his A-levels (or if he stops working 24+ hours), the eligibility rules may change

I know this isn't the answer you were hoping for, especially since Alex is working so hard managing both his studies and job. If his work situation changes and he works fewer hours, you could reapply for Child Benefit.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit eligibility, or do you have any questions about this decision?

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old and in approved education (A-levels at sixth form college on a full-time basis). However, he is working 24 or more hours per week at a supermarket. Under Child Benefit rules, children aged 16 or over who work 24+ hours per week do not qualify for Child Benefit, even if they are in approved education. Therefore, Alex does not meet the qualifying child criteria.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (17 years old), who is in approved education but works 24 or more hours per week. Due to the work hours exceeding the 24-hour threshold, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.description: For 16-19 year olds, check if in education/training or eligible for extension
response.routing_logic: If in education/training, validate type and level. If just left education (within 20 weeks), check extension eligibility.

response.question: Is the child in approved (non-advanced) education or training?
response.help_text: Approved education: A-levels, T-levels, NVQ Level 3 or below, Scottish Highers/Advanced Highers. Advanced (NOT approved): University degrees, HNC, HND, NVQ Level 4+. Must be full-time (12+ hours/week supervised study).

criteria.must_not_be_married_unless_dependent: Married 16-19 year olds only qualify if financially dependent on claimant

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.reason: Child aged 16+ is working 24 or more hours per week and not in approved education or training
response.guidance: Child Benefit stops if child aged 16 or over starts paid work for 24+ hours per week and is no longer in approved education or training. If they return to education/training, you may be able to claim again.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 17 years old and in approved education (A-levels at sixth form college on a full-time basis). However, he is working 24 or more hours per week at a supermarket. Under Child Benefit rules, children aged 16 or over who work 24+ hours per week do not qualify for Child Benefit, even if they are in approved education. Therefore, Alex does not meet the qualifying child criteria.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (17 years old), who is in approved education but works 24 or more hours per week. Due to the work hours exceeding the 24-hour threshold, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit.
